narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, without considering gender or race. In your case, the three most influential factors shaped this outcome in distinct ways. The strongest positive influence pulling toward a bar failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar, signaling to the model weaker performance in later undergraduate years compared to successful peers. On the flip side, your low family income bracket stood out as the top factor pushing strongly against failure—toward passing—since it aligns with a resilient profile seen in some who succeed despite challenges, even though low-income backgrounds are rare among bar passers (only about 1.7%). Your LSAT score of 27 also nudged the prediction toward passing, though less powerfully, as it falls below the typical 37 for those who succeed but still contributed favorably. Overall, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 and part-time enrollment plans (uncommon among the 93% full-time bar passers) played supporting roles, it was the combination—especially that low Year 3 decile overriding other strengths—that tipped the model to rejection. Compared to typical accepted students, who often have higher deciles around 6, stronger LSATs, and more full-time commitments, your profile showed promise in areas like family background resilience but gaps in key academic markers. This isn't a judgment on your potential, just the model's data-driven read. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to 37, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and exploring full-time options if feasible. Many students in similar spots have improved these metrics and succeeded—reach out to admissions for targeted advice or reapply after addressing these. Your determination, especially from a low-income background, is already a strength the model recognized. Keep pushing; paths to law school remain open.
